patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 5/5] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M

version_history.subject: [PATCH 5/5] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch limits the iterations of the `shrink_delalloc` function in Btrfs to 128M, preventing it from blocking indefinitely and causing high syscall latencies. The function is responsible for reclaiming space in the file system, but its current implementation can lead to long stalls when dealing with large amounts of delalloc data. By capping the iterations at 128M, the patch aims to strike a balance between making progress on space reclaim and avoiding arbitrary blocking. This change should improve the overall performance and responsiveness of the system, especially in scenarios where writeback is heavy and async reclaim is blocked.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 4/5] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

version_history.subject: [PATCH 4/5] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the data type of 'outstanding_extents' in the btrfs inode structure from u32 to u64. The change is motivated by the fact that the maximum file size is LLONG_MAX, which results in a bound on extent sizes that exceeds the range of a u32. The update also includes changes to the mod_outstanding_extents function to handle the new data type and ensure correctness. This patch aims to accurately represent the intent behind the types used in the code.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 3/5] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

version_history.subject: [PATCH 3/5] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue in Btrfs where the maximum number of extents reserved for delayed refs reservations during delalloc writes was under-estimated when compression is enabled. The problem arose from using a fixed estimate that didn't account for compression, leading to potential issues with reservation and release calculations. To fix this, the patch introduces new functions to calculate the maximum extent size and max extents based on inode properties, ensuring accurate estimation even in compressed scenarios. As a result, delayed refs reservations are no longer under-reserved during delalloc writes, even when compression is forced or always enabled for an inode.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/5] btrfs: account for csum delayed_refs in delalloc

version_history.subject: [PATCH 2/5] btrfs: account for csum delayed_refs in delalloc

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the btrfs delalloc space accounting to reserve space for csum delayed refs in addition to extent and free space tree refs. The update ensures a proper worst-case estimate of delayed_rsv from delalloc by mirroring the existing non-delayed-ref metadata reservation that already accounts for csums. The change involves modifying the calculation of delayed_ref bytes to include the size of the csum tree, which is generated during ordered_extent completion and cow-ed into the csum tree.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/5] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/5] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a problem where the Btrfs file system does not reserve enough space for delayed references (delayed_refs) during delalloc operations, leading to incorrect assessments of reserved metadata space and poor decision making in reclaim logic. The issue arises from the fact that the reservation is over-estimated but not meticulously tracked as each ordered extent is created. As a result, when writeback occurs and delayed refs are generated, they draw from an under-reserved trans_handle's delayed_refs_rsv. This can lead to erroneous assessments of reserved metadata space and poor decision making in reclaim logic. The patch aims to fix this by reserving space for delayed_refs in delalloc operations.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that this patch increases the size of btrfs_inode beyond 1024 bytes, which was a goal to reduce inode size down to less than 1024 bytes for better memory usage, and suggested two alternatives: adding delayed refs reservations to the existing block reserve or migrating reserved space from inode->block_rsv to trans->delayed_rsv when finishing an ordered extent.
review_comments.raw_body:
Not that long ago we had an effort to decrease the btrfs_inode
structure size down to less than 1024 bytes, so that we could have 4
inodes per 4K page instead of 3, and this change now makes the
structure larger than 1024 bytes again.

Instead of adding another block reserve to the inode we could:

1) Add the reservations for delayed refs in the existing block reserve
(inode->block_rsv);

2) When finishing the ordered extent, after joining the transaction
and setting trans->block to inode->block_rsv, we could migrate the
space reserved for delayed refs from the inode->block_rsv into
trans->delayed_rsv.

This would not require increasing the btrfs_inode structure and
neither add a _local_delayed_rsv field to the transaction handle (and
btw, we don't use the _ prefix for any structure fields anywhere in
btrfs).

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author initially expressed hesitation about tracking or re-computing the delayed_refs portion of inode->block_rsv for the migration, but then proposed an alternative approach to indirectly allocate inode->delayed_rsv using btrfs_alloc_block_rsv, which would also save space in struct btrfs_inode.
review_comments.raw_body:
At first blush, without trying very hard yet, I don't love this because I
think it means tracking or re-computing the delayed_refs portion of
inode->block_rsv for the migration. However, I'm sure I can make it work
and it's certainly worth doing to not regress the size of struct
btrfs_inode.

What do you think of just allocating inode->delayed_rsv indirectly with
btrfs_alloc_block_rsv? We can save another ~100 bytes on struct
btrfs_inode. And we could do it for inode->block_rsv to save yet more
space. Do you know if we have experience that it's really important for
inode->block_rsv to be embedded in struct btrfs_inode?

If that's a no-go, I will work on using just inode->block_rsv and doing
the migration.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Filipe Manana noted that re-computing delayed ref bytes is not expensive and can be done in a workqueue, but suggested tracking it would require increasing the structure size to at least 1024 bytes

Reviewer noted that adding an allocation for delayed refs will introduce potential -ENOMEM failures, and questioned the benefits of reserving space for them, citing specific page sizes where it would not provide significant gains.

Reviewer noted that the embedded reservation for delayed_refs has a limited benefit, only providing an advantage when the page size is greater than 4K, allowing for more inodes per page.
review_comments.raw_body:
Re-computing (calling btrfs_calc_delayed_ref_bytes()) is not
expensive, and it's done in workqueue for finishing ordered extents.
I wouldn't worry about that. But tracking it somewhere, implies
increasing the structure size - if it stays under or at 1024 bytes,
it's fine.

---

Adding an allocation adds potential for -ENOMEM failures.
And for 4K pages we will still be able to allocate 4 inodes per page,
so no gains here.
For a 64K page we can get a few more inodes (7 or 8).

---

The reason we have it embedded it's because it saves a memory
allocation, and it may also be faster and maybe better cache line
spatial locality.
In the end we won't get any benefit unless the page size is > 4K so
that we can get more inodes per page.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges that the patch's approach may be unclear and agrees to restructure in a future version (v2) to improve clarity and reduce latent state.

Author Boris Burkov is seeking clarification on whether reserving space for delayed_refs in delalloc is a general rule or specific requirement, and considering alternative approaches such as allocating block_rsvs when loading the inode or during first-time delalloc.

Author acknowledged that increasing the size of struct delayed_refs_rsv has benefits, specifically providing a buffer for future additions.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm more concerned about the code being unclear and carrying latent
state in an overloaded block_rsv. Metadata reservations are complicated
enough as it is so I was really hoping to increase clarity with the
explicit block_rsv mirroring the design of trans_handle.

---

I understand the general tradeoffs.

I was asking if we know that it's specifically important or if it's a
general rule. There are other structs in btrfs_inode that are allocated
and other allocations that occur while populating and using btrfs_inode.
(delayed_node, extent_io_tree, path/search_slot, extent_changeset in
delalloc, etc.) So we could allocate the block_rsvs in a similar way
(when loading the inode, when doing delalloc for the first time, etc.)

But I understand that you don't want to risk regressions, so I can go
ahead and do it your way if you feel it's the best way to go.

---

Well there is a benefit of more of a buffer for future additions to the
struct.

Thanks,
Boris

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/5] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/5] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve stalls in the Btrfs file system under sudden writeback conditions, particularly on systems with large amounts of memory and disk space. The problem arises when a large number of dirty pages are accumulated before writeback occurs, leading to a tailspin in metadata space management. To address this, the patches introduce improved reservations for delayed refs in delalloc work, smooth out the work in flush_space(), and reduce the size of the unit of work in shrink_delalloc(). The changes aim to prevent stalls by reducing the number of ordered extents and delayed refs blocking transactions.

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH] btrfs: fix deadlock between reflink and transaction commit when using flushoncommit
patches_acked.patch_summary: This patch fixes a deadlock between reflink and transaction commit operations in Btrfs when using the flushoncommit feature. The deadlock occurs because of a lock cycle involving three tasks: clone, commit, and writeback worker. To fix this issue, the patch modifies the locking sequence to prevent the deadlock. The modification ensures that the commit task is not blocked by the writeback worker, thus resolving the deadlock.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the original description of the deadlock scenario was incomplete and misleading due to an oversimplification of the lock cycle, which actually involves three tasks: clone, commit, and writeback worker.

Reviewer questioned the accuracy of the patch description, pointing out that the stack traces show three tasks involved in the deadlock (clone, commit, and writeback worker), not just two as described, and expressed skepticism about a scenario where the commit task is directly blocked.
review_comments.raw_body:
Well it's mentioned in step 1, and extent locking is obvious since we
have to do it for any operation that changes extents in a range, it's
not exclusive to reflinks, we do this everywhere in btrfs. I mentioned
it here in step 2 as a remainder.

---

I didn't mention the commit task waiting for the writeback worker to
simplify things.
I think anyone is able to realize the commit task blocks waiting for
the writeback worker to complete - the stack traces make that obvious.

And no, I haven't seen any case where the commit task is directly
blocked - I don't see how that could happen unless the implementation
of try_to_writeback_inodes_sb() changes and it directly does the
writeback instead of using a worker.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 00/13] nfsd/sunrpc: add support for netlink upcalls for mountd/exportd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for netlink upcalls to the NFS daemon (nfsd) and sunrpc cache mechanism, replacing antiquated interfaces in /proc. The new approach uses a generic netlink family for cache upcalls, adding new operations and attribute-sets for handling cache requests and responses.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: No substantive comments or reviews have been posted in this thread so far. The original author has posted a few messages with minor updates and rewording of comments, but no major issues or concerns have been raised.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 01/13] nfsd: move struct nfsd_genl_rqstp to nfsctl.c
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the definition of the struct nfsd_genl_rqstp from the header file nfsd.h to the source file nfsctl.c, where it is used. This simplifies the code and reduces the risk of inconsistencies between the header and source files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 02/13] sunrpc: rename sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall() to sunrpc_cache_upcall()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall() to sunrpc_cache_upcall() in preparation for its future use of netlink for upcalls, if configured. The rename is applied across four files: fs/nfsd/export.c, include/linux/sunrpc/cache.h, net/sunrpc/cache.c, and net/sunrpc/svcauth_unix.c. The patch makes 7 insertions and 7 deletions to update function calls and declarations accordingly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 03/13] sunrpc: rename sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall_timeout()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall_timeout() to sunrpc_cache_upcall_warn(). The original function name was misleading as it did not actually handle timeouts, but instead warned if there were no listeners. This change clarifies the purpose of the function and makes its name more descriptive. The rename affects 6 files in total, with 9 insertions and 9 deletions across these files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 04/13] sunrpc: rename cache_pipe_upcall() to cache_do_upcall()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function cache_pipe_upcall() to cache_do_upcall() in anticipation of a future netlink-based upcall mechanism. The renaming is done to make the code more flexible and prepare it for upcoming changes. This change does not introduce any new functionality but rather sets the stage for future improvements.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 05/13] sunrpc: add a cache_notify callback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new callback function, cache_notify, to the struct cache_detail in the sunrpc module. The purpose of this callback is to notify when there is a pending cache_request, which will be used by a later patch that sends netlink notifications for such requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 06/13] sunrpc: add helpers to count and snapshot pending cache requests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new functions, sunrpc_cache_requests_count() and sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot(), to the Linux kernel's SunRPC implementation. These functions allow callers to count and snapshot the pending upcall request list without exposing internal data structures. The count function returns the number of requests with CACHE_PENDING set, while the snapshot function takes a reference on each item so it can be safely used after the queue lock is released. This patch will be used by the nfsd generic netlink dumpit handler for svc_export upcall requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 07/13] sunrpc: add a generic netlink family for cache upcalls
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a generic netlink family called 'sunrpc' to support cache upcall notifications for sunrpc-resident caches. It defines a YAML specification, implements the necessary functions in the sunrpc module, and registers/unregisters the new netlink family during initialization and cleanup. The goal is to enable cache upcalls for the auth.unix.ip and auth.unix.gid caches that live within the sunrpc module.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 08/13] sunrpc: add netlink upcall for the auth.unix.ip cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the ip_map (auth.unix.ip) cache in the sunrpc subsystem. It introduces new attributes and operations to the sunrpc_cache YAML spec, including ip-map attribute-set and ip-map-reqs wrapper. The patch also implements two new functions: sunrpc_nl_ip_map_get_reqs_dumpit() to snapshot pending requests and send them over netlink, and sunrpc_nl_ip_map_set_reqs_doit() to parse responses from userspace and update the cache. Additionally, it wires up the ip_map_notify() callback in ip_map_cache_template to trigger SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY multicast events on cache misses.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 09/13] sunrpc: add netlink upcall for the auth.unix.gid cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for netlink-based cache upcalls for the unix_gid (auth.unix.gid) cache in the sunrpc subsystem. It introduces new attributes and operations to the sunrpc_cache YAML spec, including a new attribute-set for unix_gids and two new operations: unix-gid-get-reqs and unix-gid-set-reqs. The patch also implements netlink-based upcalls for these operations, allowing userspace to dump pending requests and respond to them. This is done by introducing new functions that snapshot pending requests, parse responses from userspace, and update the cache accordingly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 10/13] nfsd: add netlink upcall for the svc_export cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based cache upcall support to the NFS daemon (nfsd) for its svc_export cache. It introduces a new multicast event, NFSD_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY, which notifies userspace of pending requests in the cache. The patch also includes functions to snapshot and update the cache via netlink messages. This change aims to simplify the management of export options by keeping them organized in the netlink headers, reducing the need for synchronization with other parts of the codebase.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 11/13] nfsd: add netlink upcall for the nfsd.fh cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the nfsd.fh cache, allowing clients to notify the server of changes to cached file handles. The patch introduces a new 'expkey' attribute-set and operations to the nfsd YAML spec, implements netlink message handling functions, and wires up the expkey_notify callback to trigger multicast events when cache misses occur.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 12/13] sunrpc: add SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH netlink command
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH generic netlink command that allows userspace to flush the sunrpc auth caches (ip_map and unix_gid) without writing to /proc/net/rpc/*/flush. The command takes an optional attribute, SUNRPC_A_CACHE_FLUSH_MASK, which specifies which caches to flush. If omitted, all sunrpc caches are flushed. This is used by exportfs to replace its /proc-based cache_flush() with a netlink equivalent, with /proc fallback for older kernels.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new netlink command, NFSD_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH, which allows userspace to flush the nfsd export caches without writing to /proc/net/rpc/*/flush. The command takes an optional attribute, NFSD_A_CACHE_FLUSH_MASK, that specifies which caches to flush. If omitted, all nfsd caches are flushed. This is used by exportfs to replace its /proc-based cache_flush() with a netlink equivalent, and provides a fallback to /proc for older kernels.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/2] nfs: fix utimensat() for atime with delegated timestamps
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in NFS delegated timestamps where the ctime is not updated when an atime-only update is made via utimensat(). The fix involves issuing an RPC to update the ctime alongside the atime.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Posted the original patch with a description of the problem and proposed fix. The patch is a simple code change to issue an RPC when updating atime-only via utimensat().
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, 2026-03-24 at 13:32 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> xfstest generic/221 is failing with delegated timestamps enabled.  When
> the client holds a WRITE_ATTRS_DELEG delegation, and a userland process
> does a utimensat() for only the atime, the ctime is not properly
> updated. The problem is that the client tries to cache the atime update,
> but there is no mtime update, so the delegated attribute update never
> updates the ctime.
> 
> Delegated timestamps don't have a mechanism to update the ctime in
> accordance with atime-only changes due to utimensat() and the like.
> Change the client to issue an RPC in this case, so that the ctime gets
> properly updated alongside the atime.
> 
> Fixes: 40f45ab3814f ("NFS: Further fixes to attribute delegation a/mtime changes")
> Reported-by: Olga Kornievskaia <aglo@umich.edu>
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  fs/nfs/inode.c | 9 +--------
>  1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 8 deletions(-)
>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] nfs: update inode ctime after removexattr operation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the inode ctime after a removexattr operation in NFSv4.2 to ensure that the cached timestamp is refreshed. The problem it solves is that the stat() system call does not trigger a GETATTR request when delegated timestamps are used, resulting in an outdated ctime value. To fix this, the patch adds a trailing GETATTR request to the removexattr operation, similar to how setxattr operations update the inode's timestamp. This change ensures that the inode's ctime is accurately updated after removing extended attributes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a double-put bug in the NFS implementation, specifically affecting delegated attribute updates. The patches fix utimensat() for atime with delegated timestamps and update inode ctime after removexattr operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/5] io_uring/rsrc: rename io_buffer_register_bvec()/io_buffer_unregister_bvec()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames two io_uring APIs: `io_buffer_register_bvec()` and `io_buffer_unregister_bvec()`. The first is renamed to `io_buffer_register_request()` to prepare for supporting kernel-populated buffers in fuse io-uring, which will register bvecs directly. The second is renamed to `io_buffer_unregister()` to make its name more generic, as it will be used by both the new and old APIs for unregistration. This change is part of a larger effort to support kernel-populated buffers in fuse io-uring.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/5] io_uring/rsrc: split io_buffer_register_request() logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits the main initialization logic in io_buffer_register_request() into a helper function called io_kernel_buffer_init(). This is done to prepare for supporting kernel-populated buffers in fuse io-uring, which will reuse this logic. The new function initializes an io_mapped_ubuf structure and allocates memory for it. It also sets up various fields in the structure, including the buffer length, direction, and release function. The original function now calls the helper function to perform the initialization, making the code more modular and easier to maintain.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 3/5] io_uring/rsrc: add io_buffer_register_bvec()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function, io_buffer_register_bvec(), which allows registering an array of bio vectors (bvecs) to the io_uring context. The function is designed for use in fuse-over-io-uring zero-copy operations and provides a way to register multiple bvecs at once. It takes several parameters, including the io_uring command, the bvec array, and flags for issue and release. The patch also adds an inline function for the case where the feature is not supported. The new function is implemented in the io_uring/rsrc.c file and includes error handling and locking to ensure thread safety.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 4/5] io_uring/rsrc: rename and export IO_IMU_DEST / IO_IMU_SOURCE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames and exports two io_uring flags, IO_IMU_DEST and IO_IMU_SOURCE, to more descriptive names, IO_BUF_DEST and IO_BUF_SOURCE. The rename is necessary because callers may need the buffer to be both readable and writable. The flags are exported so that other subsystems can use them. The patch updates several files, including io_uring_types.h, io_uring.c, rsrc.c, and rsrc.h, making changes such as adding new enum values, updating BUILD_BUG_ON macros, and modifying code to use the new flag names.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 5/5] io_uring/rsrc: add io_uring_registered_mem_region_get()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function `io_uring_registered_mem_region_get()` to the io_uring API, which allows command handlers to retrieve the pre-registered memory region's pages and size. This is intended for use by fuse implementations to directly access pre-registered memory for passing data between the kernel and server. The function takes an io_uring command structure, a pointer to store the number of pages, and issue flags as parameters. It locks the ring context, retrieves the pre-registered memory region's pointer and page count, and then unlocks the ring context before returning the pointer. This patch provides a way for uring command implementations to directly access pre-registered memory, which is useful for reading/writing header data between the kernel and server.

review_comments.author: Jens Axboe
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Jens Axboe questioned the necessity of io_ring_submit_lock() and io_ring_submit_unlock() around io_uring_registered_mem_region_get(), suggesting that the locking should not be needed due to the stability of the ring for its duration, and proposed returning a struct instead of using a pointer for nr_pages.
review_comments.raw_body:
This looks suspicious, but I actually think it looks suspicious because
you add the submit locking around it. For patterns like that, it makes
the brain go "hmm, what protects this from going invalid the instant
io_ring_submit_unlock() is called??". But this should be stable for the
duration of the ring, hence the locking should not be needed at all?

I'd probably also prefer wrapping this in some kind of return struct, so
that you just return that rather than having one part returned directly
and one stuffed in a pointer to an unsigned for number of pages.

-- 
Jens Axboe

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed Jens Axboe's concern about the need for submit locking in io_uring_registered_mem_region_get(). The author explained that unregistration is not intended to be added/supported and that the locking is necessary to prevent partially initialized state between ptr and nr_pages. A fix is planned, but no specific changes were mentioned.

Author agreed to restructure the io_uring_registered_mem_region_get() function to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), as suggested by Jens Axboe.
sentiment_signals: acknowledged a need for a fix
review_comments.raw_body:
My understanding is that once a memory region is registered to the
ring, it's registered for the ring's lifetime. There's no uapi to
unregister a memory region and my interpretation of the last paragraph
in this thread [1] is that unregistration is not intended to be
added/supported. I think the submit locking is needed in case another
thread is currently registering it so we don't see partially
initialized state between ptr and nr_pages (eg if the caller calls
this from a task work callback).

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/000f7db7-5546-4680-bef2-84ce740ad8fd@gmail.com/

---

Sounds good, I will make this change.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Jens Axboe
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Jens Axboe requested additional comments explaining why it's safe to return the registered memory region outside the lock and why the lock is required to ensure a sane state, specifically when the region is either fully registered or not present.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes good point - can you please add a comment to that effect? Both why
it's safe to return this state outside the lock, and also why the lock
is actually required to ensure we see a sane state (either region fully
registered, or not there)?

-- 
Jens Axboe

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the submit lock is necessary for thread safety and agreed to add a comment explaining its purpose, but did not commit to making any changes yet.
review_comments.raw_body:
Good idea, I will add a comment about this to make this more clear,
something like:
/*
 * The submit lock ensures we don't see partially initialized state
 * if another thread is currently registering the region. Once registered,
 * the region is stable for the ring's lifetime (no unregister API exists),
 * so it's safe to access the returned pointer outside the lock.
 */

I will wait a day or two to send out v4 in case additional comments come in.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Mar 25, 2026 at 10:27 AM Jens Axboe <axboe@kernel.dk> wrote:

> On 3/25/26 11:24 AM, Joanne Koong wrote:

> >>> --- a/io_uring/rsrc.c

> >>>       return io_import_fixed(ddir, iter, node->buf, buf_addr, len);
> >>>  }
> >>>
> >>> +void *io_uring_registered_mem_region_get(struct io_uring_cmd *cmd,
> >>> +                                      unsigned *nr_pages,
> >>> +                                      unsigned issue_flags)
> >>> +{
> >>> +     struct io_ring_ctx *ctx = cmd_to_io_kiocb(cmd)->ctx;
> >>> +     void *ptr;
> >>> +
> >>> +     io_ring_submit_lock(ctx, issue_flags);
> >>> +
> >>> +     ptr = ctx->param_region.ptr;
> >>> +     *nr_pages = ctx->param_region.nr_pages;
> >>> +
> >>> +     io_ring_submit_unlock(ctx, issue_flags);
> >>> +

> >> This looks suspicious, but I actually think it looks suspicious because
> >> you add the submit locking around it. For patterns like that, it makes
> >> the brain go "hmm, what protects this from going invalid the instant
> >> io_ring_submit_unlock() is called??". But this should be stable for the
> >> duration of the ring, hence the locking should not be needed at all?
> >
> > My understanding is that once a memory region is registered to the
> > ring, it's registered for the ring's lifetime. There's no uapi to
> > unregister a memory region and my interpretation of the last paragraph
> > in this thread [1] is that unregistration is not intended to be
> > added/supported. I think the submit locking is needed in case another
> > thread is currently registering it so we don't see partially
> > initialized state between ptr and nr_pages (eg if the caller calls
> > this from a task work callback).
>
> Yes good point - can you please add a comment to that effect? Both why
> it's safe to return this state outside the lock, and also why the lock
> is actually required to ensure we see a sane state (either region fully
> registered, or not there)?

Good idea, I will add a comment about this to make this more clear,
something like:
/*
 * The submit lock ensures we don't see partially initialized state
 * if another thread is currently registering the region. Once registered,
 * the region is stable for the ring's lifetime (no unregister API exists),
 * so it's safe to access the returned pointer outside the lock.
 */

Joanne

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] generic/747: handle ENOSPC gracefully during write/delete cycles

version_history.subject: [PATCH] generic/747: handle ENOSPC gracefully during write/delete cycles

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a test failure issue in the generic/747 test, which consistently fails on btrfs due to ENOSPC errors during write/delete cycles. The problem arises from statfs overestimating available space at high fill levels, causing dd to hit ENOSPC even when there's room. To handle this, the patch breaks out of the fill loop when full, deletes a file to free space and retry on failure, and redirects dd stderr to preserve errors for debugging purposes. The test is designed to stress garbage collection through write/delete churn, not to validate space accounting.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Christoph Hellwig noted that the patch's ENOSPC handling may cause unexpected issues due to btrfs' statfs implementation overestimating available space, and suggested addressing this underlying filesystem bug for the benefit of users.
review_comments.raw_body:
f_bavail overestimating is a btrfs implementation bug that needs fixing.
It can be wrong, but it needs to be too low in doubt.  Without this
you're going to cause unexpected ENOSPC for real life applictions as
well (and btrfs does have a bit of a reputation for that, so fixing
this properly will benefit your users!)

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested that the issue of ENOSPC during write/delete cycles could be addressed by improving the calculation of f_bavail, rather than handling it in the test code.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested that the issue in patch generic/747 is caused by overestimation of available space due to two factors: btrfs_calc_available_data_space() not handling uneven disk spaces and total_free_data calculation not considering reserved space. He recommended trying a fix for this problem.
review_comments.raw_body:
If this is the problem, then I believe we should fix our f_bavail 
calculation.

---

From what I can see, there are several different ways that f_bavail is 
overestimated:

- btrfs_calc_available_data_space()
   That functions goes through each device and try to find unallocated
   space.
   This can not handle uneven space (e.g. 1G + 10G RAID0), and it should
   go through the  per-profile estimation, which handles uneven disks way
   better.

   Normally it shouldn't affect single device, but we can still over-
   estimate by taking the reserved 1M bytes as available.

- total_free_data calculation
   It's just on-disk size minus the on-disk used space, which doesn't
   take things like reserved space into consideration.

   Thus I guess the problem is mostly from this part.

Mind to give this idea a try to fix the long failing g747?

Thanks,
Qu

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch fixes a stale errno check in the btrfs_encoded_read and btrfs_encoded_write helpers, which can falsely trigger due to library initialization code leaving errno set to a non-zero value.

review_comments.author: Mark Harmstone
review_comments.summary: Confirmed that errno is not reset to 0 at the beginning of main, as previously assumed. Approved the patch and added a Reviewed-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 24/03/2026 7.09 pm, Leo Martins wrote:
> The atoll() error check `if (offset == 0 && errno != 0)` can falsely
> trigger because errno is not cleared before the call. Library
> initialization code (e.g. libcap calling prctl(PR_CAPBSET_READ) which
> fails with EINVAL) can leave errno set to a non-zero value. When the
> caller passes offset=0 (a valid value), atoll() returns 0 without
> modifying errno, and the stale errno causes the helper to print usage
> and exit.

Thanks Leo. From 
https://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9699919799/functions/errno.html:

 > The following sentence is deleted from the DESCRIPTION: "The value of 
errno is 0 at program start-up, but is never set to 0 by any XSI function".

So yes, apparently you can no longer rely on errno being 0 at the 
beginning of main. TIL.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] btrfs: add remap-tree checks to check_block_group_item()

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: add remap-tree checks to check_block_group_item()
[PATCH v2] btrfs: add remap-tree checks to check_block_group_item()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds write-time checks for block group items related to the remap tree in Btrfs file systems. It ensures that the REMAPPED or METADATA_REMAP flags are only set if the REMAP_TREE incompat flag is also enabled, and that `remap_bytes` and `identity_remap_count` do not exceed the size of the block group. The patch addresses a potential issue on 32-bit architectures by replacing a 64-bit division with a right shift operation.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: add BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: add BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl (input/output control) called BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS, which allows userspace to query the on-disk checksums for a file without needing to read the data itself. The ioctl takes an argument specifying the offset and length of interest, as well as a buffer to store the results. The kernel returns a struct containing information about whether the checksum is available, and if so, what type it is (e.g., sparse, preallocated, or compressed). This feature is primarily intended for speeding up mkfs.btrfs --rootdir operations, particularly when using the --reflink option, which can result in significant performance improvements.

review_comments.author: Mark Harmstone (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the patch does not address mkfs.btrfs performance and instead pointed to a separate pull request for improvements.
review_comments.raw_body:
mkfs patches for this: https://github.com/kdave/btrfs-progs/pull/1096

On 20/03/2026 12.50 pm, Mark Harmstone wrote:

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed concerns about the handling of hole and preallocated ranges in the BTRFS_GET_CSUMS_SPARSE type, suggesting that they should be treated differently due to their distinct implications for mkfs behavior. Specifically, holes should not create any extent instead of writing zero, while preallocated ranges can indicate no csums to allow mkfs to distinguish between them and change to zero writes accordingly.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested adding a special flag to indicate when csums are skipped for compressed extents, instead of using NO_CSUMS, to improve distinguishability between holes and compressed data.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested combining btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_entry and real buffer for csum into one structure, and removing redundant offset/length fields from the entry structure.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm not sure if it's a good idea to put hole and preallocated range into 
the same BTRFS_GET_CSUMS_SPARSE.

Although both means there is no csum, hole case means there is really no 
data extent, thus we should not create any extent instead of writing zero.

For preallocated, indicating it has no CSUM can allow mkfs to 
distinguish hole and preallocated, thus change to zero writes to 
prealloc, which is faster and make the resulted fs more aligned to the 
source dir.


And for EOF checks, I think we don't need to bother that much, aka, just 
let it return the regular results.

My assumption is, the mkfs shouldn't pass a range completely beyond the 
round_up(i_size), as non-reflink rootdir population would always read 
out the content of the inode from the host fs.
Thus we won't really read beyond the inode size.

---

I agree with the skip of compressed extents, but I'd prefer to have a 
special flag to indicate that, other than NO_CSUMS.

Or mkfs is unable to distinguish hole and compressed extents.

[...]

---

From the progs usage, it is always a single btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_entry 
at the beginning of buf[], then real buffer for csum, can we just 
combine both structures into one?

Furthermore, since we only query one extent at one time, the 
offset/length are more or less duplicated between args and entry structure.

We can just save the length into the args without the need for entry 
members (except the type).

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested moving hole/preallocation/compression detection from kernel space to user space by utilizing SEEK_DATA/SEEK_HOLE flags of lseek() and fiemap, which would simplify the get csum ioctl and align it with btrfs_lookup_csums_bitmap().
review_comments.raw_body:
After more investigation, I think we can put the 
hole/preallocation/compression detection into the user space.

The hole detection is already pending for merge, mostly through 
SEEK_DATA/SEEK_HOLE flags of lseek():

  https://github.com/kdave/btrfs-progs/pull/1097

I'm planning to implement preallocation detection through fiemap, which 
also allows us to detect compressed range and skip them for your case.

With all those features implemented in progs, we can further simplify 
the get csum ioctl, to something more aligned to 
btrfs_lookup_csums_bitmap().

We do not need to bother why there is no checksum for some ranges, that 
will be handled by progs first, we only need to return all the checksums 
found for the specified range.

And as an extra safenet, use some bitmap inthe ioctl structure to 
indicate which ranges have checksum and which doesn't.

This will definitely simplify the ioctl as we only need to do csum tree 
lookup, no need to bother anything in the subvolume tree.

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.author: Mark Harmstone (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged a naming issue in the ioctl type for sparse extents and agreed to rename it to something like 'ZERO' or similar, indicating no further revision planned.

The author acknowledged that returning checksums for compressed extents would leak information about other files, and explained that this is why encoded read requires root access.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks Qu.

"SPARSE" is probably a bad name for it. It probably should be "ZERO" or 
somesuch. The point is to tell userspace not to waste time calculating 
csums, but use the precomputed values because the data would be zero 
(for whatever reason).

---

Unfortunately this won't work. You have to explicitly filter out 
compressed extents, and identifying these requires checking the FS tree. 
The reason is that they may be "bookended", and you would leak 
information about other files if you returned the whole of the csums for 
the compressed extent. This is the reason why encoded read needs root.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
reviewer pointed out that the new ioctl can be implemented using fiemap, which already has a flag to indicate compressed file extents

Reviewer noted that btrfs_ioctl_get_csums() will not encounter bookend extents during a fiemap call and thus the check for BTRFS_GET_CSUMS_SPARSE is unnecessary.
review_comments.raw_body:
That's done by progs through fiemap. There will be a flag ENCODED for 
compressed file extents.

---

Nope, for the fiemap call, we will never reach any bookend extents.

Thanks,
Qu

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] btrfs: add BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch adds an ioctl to the Btrfs file system that returns precomputed checksums for files, but only if they are not compressed or sparse. The goal is to save userspace from calculating checksums unnecessarily when data is zero. However, due to the complexity of handling compressed extents, this feature requires additional filtering and root privileges.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the current approach to determine sparse or zero csums is insufficient, as it doesn't account for compressed file extents and may leak information about other files if bookended; instead suggested using fiemap with a flag ENCODED to identify such extents

Reviewer pointed out that the current implementation of BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS ioctl would leak information about other files if it returned csums for compressed extents, and suggested that explicit filtering is necessary to handle this case.
review_comments.raw_body:
That's done by progs through fiemap. There will be a flag ENCODED for 
compressed file extents.

---

Nope, for the fiemap call, we will never reach any bookend extents.

Thanks,
Qu

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 00/21] Virtual Swap Space
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces virtual swap space, a new approach to managing swap memory. It addresses lock contention issues on the virtual swap layer and reduces memory overhead by optimizing the swap descriptor size. The patches also fix various regressions reported by kernel developers and users, including deadlocks and poor swap free batching behavior.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the swap cache needs to be decoupled from physical swap infrastructure and will manage it at the virtual swap layer. They also plan to remove swap cache related helpers of swap table, repurpose the rest of the swap table as rmap (physical -> virtual swap mapping), and temporarily use a single global lock for swap cache accesses.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the swapoff path needing to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2, and provided two patches to abstract away the swapoff locking out behavior into a generic API.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the separation of logical and physical swap views by introducing two new types: swp_entry_t for virtual swap slots and swp_slot_t for physical swap slots, with an identity mapping between them. The author confirmed that no behavioral change has been made yet, but this will be used in later patches to dynamically allocate virtual swap slots.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledged that the zswap tree code can no longer be reused for the new virtual swap space design and decided to use a simple unified zswap tree in the new implementation.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the freeing ordering needs to be changed in the virtual swap slot's lifetime, agreed to restructure in v2 and explained how they plan to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap() in the swapoff path.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that memhog populating memory with 0xff triggers full overhead of swapfile-backed swap entry, which is not expected for same-filled pages. Author suggests this might be the cause of performance regression and plans to investigate further.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Mar 23, 2026 at 6:09 AM Kairui Song <ryncsn@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Sat, Mar 21, 2026 at 3:29 AM Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com> wrote:
> > This patch series is based on 6.19. There are a couple more
> > swap-related changes in mainline that I would need to coordinate
> > with, but I still want to send this out as an update for the
> > regressions reported by Kairui Song in [15]. It's probably easier
> > to just build this thing rather than dig through that series of
> > emails to get the fix patch :)
> >
> > Changelog:
> > * v4 -> v5:
> >     * Fix a deadlock in memcg1_swapout (reported by syzbot [16]).
> >     * Replace VM_WARN_ON(!spin_is_locked()) with lockdep_assert_held(),
> >       and use guard(rcu) in vswap_cpu_dead
> >       (reported by Peter Zijlstra [17]).
> > * v3 -> v4:
> >     * Fix poor swap free batching behavior to alleviate a regression
> >       (reported by Kairui Song).
>

Hi Kairui! Thanks a lot for the testing big boss :) I will focus on
the regression in this patch series - we can talk more about
directions in another thread :)

> I tested the v5 (including the batched-free hotfix) and am still
> seeing significant regressions in both sequential and concurrent swap
> workloads
>
> Thanks for the update as I can see It's a lot of thoughtful work.
> Actually I did run some tests already with your previously posted
> hotfix based on v3. I didn't update the result because very
> unfortunately, I still see a major performance regression even with a
> very simple setup.
>
> BTW there seems a simpler way to reproduce that, just use memhog:
> sudo mkswap /dev/pmem0; sudo swapon /dev/pmem0; time memhog 48G; sudo swapoff -a
>
> Before:
> (I'm using fish shell on that test machine so this is fish time format):
> ________________________________________________________
> Executed in   20.80 secs    fish           external
>    usr time    5.14 secs    0.00 millis    5.14 secs
>    sys time   15.65 secs    1.17 millis   15.65 secs
> ________________________________________________________
> Executed in   21.69 secs    fish           external
>    usr time    5.31 secs  725.00 micros    5.31 secs
>    sys time   16.36 secs  579.00 micros   16.36 secs
> ________________________________________________________
> Executed in   21.86 secs    fish           external
>    usr time    5.39 secs    1.02 millis    5.39 secs
>    sys time   16.46 secs    0.27 millis   16.46 secs
>
> After:
> ________________________________________________________
> Executed in   30.77 secs    fish           external
>    usr time    5.16 secs  767.00 micros    5.16 secs
>    sys time   25.59 secs  580.00 micros   25.59 secs
> ________________________________________________________
> Executed in   37.47 secs    fish           external
>    usr time    5.48 secs    0.00 micros    5.48 secs
>    sys time   31.98 secs  674.00 micros   31.98 secs
> ________________________________________________________
> Executed in   31.34 secs    fish           external
>    usr time    5.22 secs    0.00 millis    5.22 secs
>    sys time   26.09 secs    1.30 millis   26.09 secs
>
> It's obviously a lot slower.
>
> pmem may seem rare but SSDs are good at sequential, and memhog uses
> the same filled page and backend like ZRAM has extremely low overhead
> for same filled pages. Results with ZRAM are very similar, and many
> production workloads have massive amounts of samefill memory.
>
> For example on the Android phone I'm using right now at this moment:
> # cat /sys/block/zram0/mm_stat
> 4283899904 1317373036 1370259456        0 1475977216   116457  1991851
>    87273  1793760
> ~450M of samefill page in ZRAM, we may see more on some server
> workload. And I'm seeing similar memhog results with ZRAM, pmem is
> just easier to setup and less noisy. also simulates high speed
> storage.

Interesting. Normally "lots of zero-filled page" is a very beneficial
case for vswap. You don't need a swapfile, or any zram/zswap metadata
overhead - it's a native swap backend. If production workload has this
many zero-filled pages, I think the numbers of vswap would be much
less alarming - perhaps even matching memory overhead because you
don't need to maintain a zram entry metadata (it's at least 2 words
per zram entry right?), while there's no reverse map overhead induced
(so it's 24 bytes on both side), and no need to do zram-side locking
:)

So I was surprised to see that it's not working out very well here. I
checked the implementation of memhog - let me know if this is wrong
place to look:

https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man8/memhog.8.html
https://github.com/numactl/numactl/blob/master/memhog.c#L52

I think this is what happened here: memhog was populating the memory
0xff, which triggers the full overhead of a swapfile-backed swap entry
because even though it's "same-filled" it's not zero-filled! I was
following Usama's observation - "less than 1% of the same-filled pages
were non-zero" - and so I only handled the zero-filled case here:

https://lore.kernel.org/all/20240530102126.357438-1-usamaarif642@gmail.com/

This sounds a bit artificial IMHO - as Usama pointed out above, I
think most samefilled pages are zero pages, in real production
workloads. However, if you think there are real use cases with a lot
of non-zero samefilled pages, please let me know I can fix this real
quick. We can support this in vswap with zero extra metadata overhead
- change the VSWAP_ZERO swap entry type to VSWAP_SAME_FILLED, then use
the backend field to store that value. I can send you a patch if
you're interested.

>
> I also ran the previous usemem matrix, which seems better than V3 but
> still pretty bad:
> Test: usemem --init-time -O -n 1 56G, 16G mem, 48G swap, avgs of 8 run.
> Before:
> Throughput (Sum): 528.98 MB/s Throughput (Mean): 526.113333 MB/s Free
> Latency: 3037932.888889
> After:
> Throughput (Sum): 453.74 MB/s Throughput (Mean): 454.875000 MB/s Free
> Latency: 5001144.500000 (~10%, 64% slower)
>
> I'm not sure why our results differ so much — perhaps different LRU
> settings, memory pressure ratios, or THP/mTHP configs? Here's my exact
> config in the attachment. Also includes the full log and info, with
> all debug options disabled for close to production. I ran it 8 times
> and just attached the first result log, it's all similar anyway, my
> test framework reboot the machine after each test run to reduce any
> potential noise.

Ohh interesting - I see that you're testing with MGLRU. I can give that a try.

I'm not enabling THP/mTHP, but I don't see that you're enabling it
either - there's some 2MB swpout but that seems incidental.

Another difference is the swap backend:

1. Regarding pmem backend - I'm not sure if I can get my hands on one
of these, but if you think SSD has the same characteristics maybe I
can give that a try? The problem with SSD is for some reason variance
tends to be pretty high, between iterations yes, but especially across
reboots. Or maybe zram?

2. What about the other numbers below? Are they also on pmem? FTR I
was running most of my benchmarks on zswap, except for one kernel
build benchmark on SSD.

3. Any other backends and setup you're interested in?

BTW, sounds like you have a great benchmark suite - is it open source
somewhere? If not, can you share it with us :) Vswap aside, I think
this would be a good suite to run all swap related changes for every
swap contributor.

Once again, thank you so much for your engagement, Kairui. Very much
appreciated - I owe you a beverage of your choice whenever we meet.
And have a great rest of your day :)

No, that was intentional :) I probably should have documented this
better - but we're only charging towards swap usage (cgroup and system
wide) on memory. There was a whole patch that did that in the series
:)

I can add new counters to differentiate these cases, but it makes no
sense to me to charge towards swap usage for non-swapfile backend
(namely, zswap and zero swap pages). You are not actually occupying
the limited swapfile slots, but instead occupy a dynamic, vast virtual
swap space only (and memory in the case of zswap - this is actually an
argument against zram which does not do any cgroup accounting, but
that's another story for another day). I don't see a point in swap
charging here. It's the whole point of decoupling the backends - these
are not the same resource domains.

And if you follow Usama's work above, we actually were trying to

We couldn't fix the race in a way that does not induce more overhead
than it's worth. But had that work we would also not do any swap
charging :)

On Tue, Mar 24, 2026 at 9:19 AM Askar Safin <safinaskar@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>:
> > We can even perform compressed writeback
> > (i.e writing these pages without decompressing them) (see [12]).
>
> > [12]: https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/ZeZSDLWwDed0CgT3@casper.infradead.org/
>
> This is supported in zram. The support was added here:
> https://lore.kernel.org/all/20251201094754.4149975-1-senozhatsky@chromium.org/ .
> It is already in mainline.

I'm aware of that work. It's an improvement, but my understanding is:

1. It only works for zram.

2. We still occupy the full PAGE_SIZE slot.

3. The writeback IO request is still of size PAGE_SIZE.

So we're saving the CPU work for decompression, but not the rest of
the potential benefits of compressed writeback.

For zswap, decoupling zswap and disk swap is a pre-requisite
(otherwise every zswap slot occupy a PAGE_SIZE slot in the swapfile
anyway).

Then, we have two alternatives. Either we implement a small-slot
allocator for swapfile-infra, or we writeback a full backing page for
compressed memory. The second option is a bit more straightforward,
but then we lose relative age of these objects - a backing page might
combine very recent compressed pages and very old compressed pages.

These approaches have different performance tradeoffs and need to be
evaluated. But anyway this is future work.

It's obviously a lot slower.

pmem may seem rare but SSDs are good at sequential, and memhog uses
the same filled page and backend like ZRAM has extremely low overhead
for same filled pages. Results with ZRAM are very similar, and many
production workloads have massive amounts of samefill memory.

For example on the Android phone I'm using right now at this moment:

I also ran the previous usemem matrix, which seems better than V3 but

settings, memory pressure ratios, or THP/mTHP configs? Here's my exact
config in the attachment. Also includes the full log and info, with
all debug options disabled for close to production. I ran it 8 times
and just attached the first result log, it's all similar anyway, my
test framework reboot the machine after each test run to reduce any
potential noise.

And the above tests are only about sequential performance, concurrent
ones seem worse:

And I double checked I'm testing your latest V5 commit here:
commit 9114ebedb82089ebd3519854964c73d3959b10c0 (HEAD -> upstream/vswap)
Author: Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>
Date:   Fri Mar 20 12:27:35 2026 -0700

    vswap: batch contiguous vswap free calls

    In vswap_free(), we release and reacquire the cluster lock for every
    single entry, even for non-disk-swap backends where the lock drop is
    unnecessary. Batch consecutive free operations to avoid this overhead.

    Signed-off-by: Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>

The two kernels being tested:
/boot/vmlinuz-6.19.0.orig-g05f7e89ab973
/boot/vmlinuz-6.19.0.ptch-g9114ebedb820



These tests above are done with an EPYC 7K62, I also setup an Intel
8255C with fresh installed upstream Fedora, and using Fedora's kernel
config. So far the result matches, the gap seems smaller but still
>20% slower for many cases, so this is a common problem:

3 test run on 8255C using fresh installed Fedora and Fedora kernel config:
taskset -c 3 /usr/local/bin/usemem --init-time -O -n 1 112G
(That's a two nodes large machine so I pin the thread on CPU 3 for stability)

Before:

2157355 usecs to free memory

135291469824 bytes / 127248347 usecs = 1038289 KB/s
3035986 usecs to free memory

And beside these known cases, my main concern is still that a
mandatory virtual layer seems just wrong, it changes how swap work in
many ways. Storage folks have been trying to bypass the kernel for
decades, as abstraction layers come with overhead — that's common
knowledge. Swap lives right at the intersection of storage and mm and
has to stay inside the kernel, so we really want the kernel path to be
as flat and direct as possible.

I'm also worried this risks undoing all the recent and upcoming work
for reducing memory usage and performance. We've been trying to shrink
per-entry overhead (I'm already feeling nervous over the current
8-byte per-entry cost, and hope soon we'll get down to <1–3 bytes).
The series mentions 24 bytes of overhead, but when I account for the
reverse mapping, it looks >32 bytes per entry.

The intermediate large XArray layer also worries me as the swap space
is now very large. The virtual size could grow with no limit. e.g. a 1
TB swap space would be a 4 layers radix tree, increasing global
contention (int(1024 * 1024 / 2) >> 6 >> 6 >> 6 == 2) and vswap could
be even larger if fragmentation happens. That's the exact problem the
old sub-address_space design for SWAP was created to solve. We only
eliminated that complexity a few months ago, and this approach seems
like it would have to bring a similar structure back to reduce
contention.

And for swapoff support: minor anonymous faults during busy periods
are indeed critical for some workloads, and being able to swapoff
cleanly is still very useful both for performance and troubleshooting.
You will need to touch many things to solve a minor fault.

For reference, I've been exploring an approach that keeps the virtual
layer runtime-optional, which avoids these overheads for workloads
that don't need virtualization:
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260220-swap-table-p4-v1-0-104795d19815@tencent.com/

I vaguely remember some workloads like Java or some JS engine
